docs.title: Hello, I'm Supratim 👋
docs.text:
Data Science and AI Leader · Product and System Thinker

Two decades of turning data into decisions and ideas into intelligent products.

docs.title: What I Do
docs.text:
I build AI-driven products and platforms that solve real problems at scale - from predictive analytics engines to enterprise ML pipelines. My strength is bridging strategy and execution across AI, engineering, and product.

AI and Machine Learning Models that move the needle and create an impact - Generative and Agentic AI, predictive and propensity models, recommendation systems, and more.

Data Science at Scale End-to-end pipelines, experimentation frameworks, and analytics that drive decisions.

Product Thinking Turning ambiguous problems into packaged and shipped solutions.

docs.text:
Commonwealth Bank Leading AI initiatives in Data & Decision Science - making banking brighter for millions Cricket.com Built Criclytics - a prediction engine delivering industry-leading match forecasting accuracy Oracle Architected hybrid cloud + billing, order and revenue management, and predictive analytics solutions across telecom clients globally Amdocs Led engineering teams shipping activation and revenue management products

docs.title: Work Experience
docs.text: Two decades building data-driven products, AI platforms, and enterprise solutions across banking, sports-tech, cloud, and telecom.

docs.title: Commonwealth Bank of Australia
docs.text:
Senior Manager — Data & Decision Science · Dec 2021 – Present

Bengaluru, India

One of the earliest members of the Data & Decision Science team at CBA India. My key contributions:

  • Built the Data Science function in India from the ground up - hired a high-performing team and established a culture of rigorous, impact-driven work.
  • Leading multiple strategic Generative and Agentic AI and Macine Learning initiatives to improve how we work and deliver better outcomes for customers.
    • Built an automated framework for development of propensity models for marketing teams - a single pipeline from data ingestion to model development and deployment - reducing the time to market from 8 weeks to 2 weeks. Featured in IT News Australia
    • Built an in-house Generative-AI driven Intelligent Document Processing (IDP) product to automate the extraction of information from unstructured documents - enabling straight through processing of key customer facing processes. Featured in CIO Inc
    • Driving adoption of Generative and Agentic AI across the bank.

docs.title: Head Digital Works Pvt. Ltd.
docs.text:
Cricket.com · Sep 2019 – Dec 2021 · Bengaluru, India

Note

Due to regulatory guidelines, few products including cricket.com has shut down its operations in late 2025.

Lead Data Scientist · Sep 2019 – Dec 2021

  • Established and led the Data Science team of cricket.com.
  • Built Criclytics - an industry-leading prediction engine for match outcomes and real-time insights.
  • Introduced the Fantasy Research Centre - automated and optimised fantasy team strategies using Linear Optimisation.
  • Developed models for performance forecasting, player and team Elo ratings, post-match analysis, and playoff simulations.
  • Created a content recommendation system - enhanceed user engagement by 40%.
  • Delivered end-to-end ML pipelines and deployed models as scalable APIs.
  • Contributed to user and marketing analytics through data pipelines and A/B testing.

docs.title: Oracle
docs.text:
10 yrs 6 mos · Bengaluru · New York · St. Louis

Consulting Technical Manager · Mar 2019 – Sep 2019

  • Lead Architect for a Hybrid Cloud Solution with Predictive Analytics, integrating CX on cloud with on-premise order orchestration, service fulfilment, monetisation, and analytics for the communications industry.

Senior Principal Consultant · Nov 2015 – Feb 2019

  • Led end-to-end architecture, business process framework, and ML framework design.
  • Built models for customer segmentation (clustering) and churn prediction (classification) to improve experience and retention.

Principal Consultant · Oct 2012 – Oct 2015

  • Led development and delivery of solutions on Oracle's RODOD (Rapid Offer Design & Order Delivery) framework.

Senior Consultant · Apr 2009 – Sep 2012

  • Development and delivery of solutions on Oracle BRM (Billing & Revenue Management).

docs.title: Amdocs
docs.text:
Senior Programming Subject Matter Expert · Jul 2005 – Mar 2009

Limassol, Cyprus

  • Development Team Lead for Amdocs Engineering and Implementation Teams.
  • Worked on Amdocs Activation Manager (AAM) and Partner Revenue Management (PRM) products.

docs.title: UshaComm India Pvt. Ltd.
docs.text:
Software Developer · Jun 2003 – Jul 2005

Kolkata, India

  • Core Development Team member for Unicorn 6.0 product.
  • Focused on Inventory Management, Dealer Management, and System Administrator modules.

docs.title: April 2026
docs.text: • 2026-04-17 — 9 articles curated

docs.title: Blogs and Articles
docs.text: Insights, learnings, and analysis from my journey in data science, AI and software engineering.

docs.title: What's Up With Traffic on the Roads of Bangalore?
docs.text:
A visual analysis of traffic-related incidents on the roads of Bangalore — uncovering patterns, hotspots, and insights from accident data.

This post explores accident data on Bangalore's roads, using data analysis and visualization techniques to understand where, when, and why traffic incidents occur most frequently.

Read the full article

docs.title: Neural Network with TensorFlow: How to Stop Training Using Callback?
docs.text:
A practical tutorial on using callbacks to control training in TensorFlow — stopping early when the desired accuracy is reached.

Often, when training a very deep neural network, we want to stop training once the model reaches a target accuracy or when the loss stops improving. TensorFlow provides a clean mechanism for this via callbacks.

This tutorial walks through how to implement custom callbacks to gain fine-grained control over your training loop.

Read the full tutorial

docs.title: Batsmen in CPL: A Cluster Analysis
docs.text:
A mathematical analysis of batsmen performances in the Caribbean Premier League — grouping players by their playing style and effectiveness using clustering techniques.

A mathematical analysis throws up some interesting results on how CPL batsmen can be grouped based on their performance metrics. Using clustering algorithms, we uncover distinct playing styles and identify which cluster a batsman belongs to.

Read the full article

docs.title: 5 Key Lessons from Execution of Projects in Data Science
docs.text:
A gist of what I learnt over the years from executing many projects in data science — practical takeaways that go beyond algorithms.

Data science is as much about execution as it is about algorithms. This post distills the key lessons I've picked up from years of building and shipping data science projects — from scoping and stakeholder management to model deployment and iteration.

Read the full article

docs.title: A Programmer’s Journey, From Pointers to Python
docs.text:
“It’s harder to read code than to write it.” — Joel Spolsky

As I reflect on my two-decade journey in programming and the impact of languages on my career evolution and overall landscape of technology, I am fascinated not only by the steady evolution of paradigm and syntax but its impact on our thought and approach to designs. From early days as a Software Developer whose world was painted in C to my current role as a Data Scientist living and dreaming in Python — the journey had been more than just of code snippets, it was a voyage of technological evolution.

Read the full article

docs.title: The Discipline of Testing in the Age of Machine Learning
docs.text:
Published · March 28, 2026

What Fred Brooks knew that machine learning is still learning

Back in 1975, Fred Brooks, in his seminal work "The Mythical Man-Month" wrote - dedicate half the schedule for "testing". His was not a suggestion, but a rule of thumb from decades of hard experience building real software.

Fred Brooks' rule of thumb for scheduling — The Mythical Man-Month (1975)

docs.title: When Testing Was Non-Negotiable
docs.text:
Early in my career as a software engineer, even though we were always attracted toward the exciting part of development i.e. "coding", we gradually realized the importance of designing and executing tests, along with good documentation. It wasn't the most glamorous work, but it was the work that determined whether you could trust what you built.

That rigor is largely missing in machine learning. Not because people don't care, but because the problem is genuinely harder than anything Brooks was describing.

docs.title: The Contract Is Clear
docs.text: In traditional software, the logic is explicit. You write a function, you know what it should return, and you test against that. If it does not work, you debug and fix the logic. In ML, the behavior is learned, not written. The model encodes patterns from data in ways that even its creators don't fully understand. There is no fixed output to assert against - only probabilities, distributions, and behavior that can quietly shift when the underlying world does.

docs.title: How ML Tests - And Where It Falls Short
docs.text: So ML found its own version of testing - hold out some data the model hasn't seen, measure accuracy (and other metrics relevant to the problem), and try to estimate what that means for the business. It definitely works up to a point. But out-of-time sample performance is a proxy, not a guarantee. And business impact is even harder to pin down, because models can look right on every metric and still move the wrong needle in production.

docs.title: When There Is No Right Answer
docs.text:
Benchmarks, adversarial testing, drift detection - these are serious attempts at a hard problem. But they are still uneven and nowhere close to the shared discipline that software engineering has built over decades.

With systems built on LLMs the problem goes even further, because there is often no single correct output to benchmark against at all. Building that benchmark is a big challenge in itself. So it is often just degrees of better or worse. And that is a whole new level of unsolved!

docs.title: The Discipline Will Come - It Always Does
docs.text: I think that discipline in software engineering did not emerge because the industry was thoughtful, rather because enough things broke in production, badly enough that there was no longer a choice. Predictive models has been in production long enough, and the pain is real. Perhaps we are finally beginning to take seriously what Brooks told us half a century ago.

docs.title: A Problem Well Stated is a Problem Half Solved
docs.text:
Published · Aptil 03, 2026

"A problem well stated is a problem half solved." — Charles Kettering

More often than not, issues in software do not arise because the code is wrong. They come from incomplete thinking behind the code. When the problem is understood clearly, the solution almost always becomes simpler.

docs.title: We Start With the How
docs.text:
In most projects, the first discussions are about how to build something - tools, architecture, timelines, design and so on. Very quickly, the team starts writing code even though the problem is not fully understood.

This creates a gap between what is built and what was actually needed. And that gap is rarely visible until it is too late.

docs.title: Requirements Are Not the Same as Understanding
docs.text:
Most requirements describe features. They say what the system should do, but not how it will be used in real life. Who will use it, how their needs might evolve, what edge cases will emerge when it goes live - these questions get less attention than they deserve.

The code may be correct, but the system still feels wrong because the thinking behind it was incomplete. This is one of the most common and most expensive problems in software development, and it rarely shows up in a bug report.

docs.title: Good Engineering Starts Before Coding
docs.text:
Good engineering begins with simple questions.

  • What is the real problem?
  • Why does this need to exist?
  • How will this actually be used when it is live?

When these questions are answered clearly, the design becomes simpler, the code becomes easier to write, and the system becomes easier to maintain and change later as needed. Most of what makes software hard to maintain is not complexity in the code, but the gaps in the understanding that the code was built on.

The "how" matters. But the "what" and the "why" matter more.

docs.title: Project: AI-Driven Product Platform
docs.text: Category: AI/ML Product Technologies: Python, TensorFlow, FastAPI, React Impact: 40% improvement in user engagement through intelligent personalization

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Developed an intelligent product recommendation platform that leverages machine learning to deliver personalized user experiences at scale, serving 10M+ users daily.

docs.title: Recommendation Engine
docs.text:
• Collaborative filtering with matrix factorization
  • Content-based filtering using embeddings
  • Hybrid approach combining both techniques
  • Real-time inference with <100ms latency

docs.title: Machine Learning Pipeline
docs.text:
• Feature engineering using user and item interactions
  • Model training with continuous online learning
  • A/B testing framework for model evaluation
  • Automated retraining pipeline

docs.title: API Layer
docs.text:
• FastAPI for high-performance REST API
  • GraphQL for flexible querying
  • Redis caching for performance
  • Rate limiting and security

docs.title: Results & Impact
docs.text:
• 40% improvement in click-through rate
  • 35% increase in conversion rate
  • 25% improvement in user retention
  • Scaled to 10M+ daily active users

docs.title: Technical Excellence
docs.text:
• Clean, Pythonic code architecture
  • Comprehensive test coverage (>90%)
  • Detailed comments and documentation
  • Microservices-based design
  • Kubernetes orchestration for scaling

docs.title: Project: Criclytics Platform
docs.text: Category: Sports Analytics Technologies: Python, ML, Real-time Analytics, Vue.js Impact: Served millions of cricket enthusiasts with industry-leading prediction accuracy

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Criclytics is a cutting-edge predictive analytics platform that delivers real-time cricket predictions with industry-leading accuracy. The platform combines advanced machine learning models with comprehensive cricket statistics to provide actionable insights.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Real-time Predictions - Live match predictions updated every ball
  • Statistical Models - Advanced ML models trained on historical cricket data
  • User Insights - Personalized cricket analytics and recommendations
  • Performance Tracking - Monitor model accuracy and predictions

docs.title: Data Pipeline
docs.text:
• Ingested multiple data sources including live cricket feeds
  • Processed millions of data points daily
  • Implemented streaming architecture for real-time updates

docs.title: Machine Learning
docs.text:
• Built ensemble models combining multiple ML techniques
  • Achieved 85%+ prediction accuracy
  • Implemented model versioning and A/B testing

docs.title: Frontend
docs.text:
• Responsive web interface built with Vue.js
  • Real-time data visualization using D3.js
  • Mobile-friendly responsive design

docs.title: Results
docs.text:
• Achieved 85%+ prediction accuracy on match outcomes
  • Served 10M+ cricket enthusiasts monthly
  • Maintained 99.9% platform uptime
  • Reduced prediction latency to <500ms

docs.title: Project: Real-time Data Pipeline
docs.text: Category: Data Engineering Technologies: Python, Apache Kafka, Spark, Hadoop, PostgreSQL Impact: Processing petabyte-scale data with sub-second latency

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Built a high-performance real-time data pipeline for enterprise scale analytics, processing millions of events daily while maintaining sub-second latency and 99.99% reliability.

docs.title: Data Ingestion Layer
docs.text:
• Apache Kafka for event streaming
  • Multi-datacenter deployment for redundancy
  • Support for 100K+ events per second

docs.title: Stream Processing
docs.text:
• Apache Spark Streaming for real-time aggregations
  • Windowed operations for time-series analytics
  • Exactly-once processing semantics

docs.title: Storage Layer
docs.text:
• PostgreSQL for relational data
  • Hadoop HDFS for archival storage
  • Redis for hot cache

docs.title: Performance Metrics
docs.text:
• Throughput: 1M+ events per second
  • Latency: <500ms end-to-end
  • Reliability: 99.99% uptime
  • Data Volume: 10TB+ daily ingestion

docs.title: Technologies & Best Practices
docs.text:
• Python for all data processing logic
  • Containerization with Docker for deployment
  • Infrastructure as Code using Terraform
  • Comprehensive monitoring with Prometheus and Grafana
  • Extensive logging and tracing for debugging
